clusters.title: Short Bursts of Intense Exercise Linked to Lower Disease Risk
clusters.summary: A recent study involving nearly 100,000 participants found that engaging in just a few minutes of vigorous physical activity each day can significantly reduce the risk of developing eight major diseases, including heart disease and dementia. The research highlights the importance of exercise intensity over duration, suggesting that short, intense workouts may be more beneficial for health outcomes.

items.title: Just a few minutes of effort could lower your risk of 8 major diseases
items.description: Just a few minutes of getting out of breath each day could dramatically cut your risk of major diseases—including heart disease, dementia, and diabetes. A large study of nearly 100,000 people found that it’s not just how much you move, but how intensely you move that matters. Short bursts of vigorous activity—like rushing for a bus or climbing stairs quickly—were linked to striking reductions in disease risk, especially for inflammatory conditions and brain health.

items.title: Exercise harder—not just longer—to reduce risk of disease and death, says study
items.description: People who get just a few minutes of vigorous activity daily are less likely to develop eight major diseases, including arthritis, heart disease and dementia, according to research published in the European Heart Journal. The researchers carried out a detailed study of around 96,000 people, comparing their overall activity levels with the amount of vigorous activity and their subsequent risk of eight major diseases.

clusters.title: When crying doesn't bring relief
clusters.summary: Crying is often seen as a healthy emotional release. However, a new study suggests that the picture is more complex. In a four-week smartphone-based study, researchers found that crying did not generally make people feel better.

items.title: When crying doesn't bring relief
items.description: Crying is often seen as a healthy emotional release. However, a new study suggests that the picture is more complex. In a four-week smartphone-based study, researchers found that crying did not generally make people feel better. Instead, its short-term emotional impact varied depending on the reason for crying. Crying after situations of emotional strain or feeling overwhelmed was linked to lower positive affect and higher negative affect, while crying in response to moving media content was associated with a reduction in negative affect.

items.title: Does crying actually make you feel better? New psychology research shows it depends on a key factor
items.description: A recent study published in Collabra: Psychology suggests that crying does not universally improve a person's mood. Instead, the emotional aftermath of shedding tears depends almost entirely on the specific reasons behind the crying episode.

clusters.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
clusters.summary: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling.

items.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
items.description: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling. Encouraging results have led researchers to launch a larger Phase 3 trial.

clusters.title: Autism risk genes are shared across ancestries, research reveals
clusters.summary: A new study, co-led by researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai and published March 30 in Nature Medicine, demonstrates that genes associated with autism risk are largely the same across people of different ancestries. The findings, based on one of the largest genomic studies of Latin American individuals to date, provide strong evidence that the genetic architecture of autism is consistent across diverse populations. They underscore the importance of expanding genetic research beyond individuals of European ancestry.

items.title: Autism risk genes are shared across ancestries, research reveals
items.description: A new study, co-led by researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai and published March 30 in Nature Medicine, demonstrates that genes associated with autism risk are largely the same across people of different ancestries. The findings, based on one of the largest genomic studies of Latin American individuals to date, provide strong evidence that the genetic architecture of autism is consistent across diverse populations. They underscore the importance of expanding genetic research beyond individuals of European ancestry.

clusters.title: Insulin pills may soon replace daily injections
clusters.summary: For over a century, scientists have chased the dream of insulin pills, but the digestive system kept destroying the drug before it could work—forcing millions of patients to rely on daily injections. Now, researchers at Kumamoto University have developed a clever workaround using a tiny peptide that helps insulin slip through the intestinal wall.

items.title: Insulin pills may soon replace daily injections
items.description: For over a century, scientists have chased the dream of insulin pills, but the digestive system kept destroying the drug before it could work—forcing millions of patients to rely on daily injections. Now, researchers at Kumamoto University have developed a clever workaround using a tiny peptide that helps insulin slip through the intestinal wall.

clusters.title: After allogeneic transplant, VIC-1911 regimen shows low severe GVHD and no relapse
clusters.summary: Results of a Phase I clinical trial show that patients who undergo a blood stem cell transplant involving a donor have a lower risk of relapse and lower rates of graft-versus-host disease when they receive the targeted therapy VIC-1911 along with the standard-of-care regimen that includes post-transplant cyclophosphamide and sirolimus.

items.title: After allogeneic transplant, VIC-1911 regimen shows low severe GVHD and no relapse
items.description: Results of a Phase I clinical trial show that patients who undergo a blood stem cell transplant involving a donor have a lower risk of relapse and lower rates of graft-versus-host disease when they receive the targeted therapy VIC-1911 along with the standard-of-care regimen that includes post-transplant cyclophosphamide and sirolimus.

clusters.title: Fathers face rising depression risk a year after baby arrives
clusters.summary: New fathers appear to have fewer mental health diagnoses during pregnancy and the early months after birth. But that early stability does not last. About a year later, depression and stress-related disorders increase significantly, surprising researchers.

items.title: Fathers face rising depression risk a year after baby arrives
items.description: New fathers appear to have fewer mental health diagnoses during pregnancy and the early months after birth. But that early stability does not last. About a year later, depression and stress-related disorders increase significantly, surprising researchers. The findings suggest that the emotional toll of fatherhood builds over time rather than hitting immediately.

clusters.title: Perceived partner effort may increase post-error slowing in a shared attention task
clusters.summary: In human sociability, cooperation occupies a central place. Compared to other species, humans cooperate with one another in a more flexible manner and across a wider range of contexts. This pro-social disposition leads us to contribute to others' goals and to shared goals, often setting aside immediate interests to benefit partners and broader social groups.

items.title: Perceived partner effort may increase post-error slowing in a shared attention task
items.description: In human sociability, cooperation occupies a central place. Compared to other species, humans cooperate with one another in a more flexible manner and across a wider range of contexts. This pro-social disposition leads us to contribute to others' goals and to shared goals, often setting aside immediate interests to benefit partners and broader social groups.

clusters.title: Scientists just found the brain’s hidden defense against Alzheimer’s
clusters.summary: A new study has uncovered why some brain cells are more resistant to Alzheimer’s damage than others. Researchers found a natural cleanup system that helps remove toxic tau protein before it can form harmful clumps. The study also shows that cellular stress can produce a dangerous tau fragment linked to Alzheimer’s.

items.title: Scientists just found the brain’s hidden defense against Alzheimer’s
items.description: A new study has uncovered why some brain cells are more resistant to Alzheimer’s damage than others. Researchers found a natural cleanup system that helps remove toxic tau protein before it can form harmful clumps. The study also shows that cellular stress can produce a dangerous tau fragment linked to Alzheimer’s. Strengthening the brain’s natural defenses could point the way to new treatments.

clusters.title: Electronic dance music events appear to provide a mental health boost for women over 40
clusters.summary: A new study explores why female music fans over 40 continue to attend dance clubs and festivals. Despite facing occasional social judgments, older women report that electronic dance music greatly improves their mental and physical well-being.

items.title: Electronic dance music events appear to provide a mental health boost for women over 40
items.description: A new study explores why female music fans over 40 continue to attend dance clubs and festivals. Despite facing occasional social judgments, older women report that electronic dance music greatly improves their mental and physical well-being.

clusters.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
clusters.summary: After a stroke, the brain may do something surprisingly hopeful—it can “refresh” parts of itself. Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.

items.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
items.description: After a stroke, the brain may do something surprisingly hopeful—it can “refresh” parts of itself. Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.

clusters.title: Scientists say most of what you do each day happens on autopilot
clusters.summary: Most of our daily actions may happen without much thought. Researchers found that around 65% of everyday behaviors are triggered automatically by habit rather than conscious decisions. Many of these habits actually support our personal goals, helping us follow through on things like healthy routines.

items.title: Scientists say most of what you do each day happens on autopilot
items.description: Most of our daily actions may happen without much thought. Researchers found that around 65% of everyday behaviors are triggered automatically by habit rather than conscious decisions. Many of these habits actually support our personal goals, helping us follow through on things like healthy routines. The key to lasting change, scientists say, is building new positive habits while disrupting the cues that trigger bad ones.

clusters.title: New pill cuts “bad” cholesterol by 60% in major trial
clusters.summary: A new pill, enlicitide, reduced LDL (“bad”) cholesterol by about 60% in a large clinical trial, matching the power of injectable therapies. Because it’s taken orally, it could overcome one of the biggest barriers keeping patients from using current treatments. Researchers say many people still don’t reach safe cholesterol levels—even on statins—highlighting the need for better options.

items.title: New pill cuts “bad” cholesterol by 60% in major trial
items.description: A new pill, enlicitide, reduced LDL (“bad”) cholesterol by about 60% in a large clinical trial, matching the power of injectable therapies. Because it’s taken orally, it could overcome one of the biggest barriers keeping patients from using current treatments. Researchers say many people still don’t reach safe cholesterol levels—even on statins—highlighting the need for better options.

clusters.title: The brain remembers: The hidden cost of young adult substance use
clusters.summary: Young adults who heavily use substances may report significantly poorer memory decades later, a new University of Michigan study suggests. Researchers tracked how frequently participants reported binge drinking and daily—or near-daily—use of alcohol, cannabis and cigarettes between the ages of 18 and 30. They then compared those patterns with self-reported poor memory at ages 50 to 65.

items.title: The brain remembers: The hidden cost of young adult substance use
items.description: Young adults who heavily use substances may report significantly poorer memory decades later, a new University of Michigan study suggests. Researchers tracked how frequently participants reported binge drinking and daily—or near-daily—use of alcohol, cannabis and cigarettes between the ages of 18 and 30. They then compared those patterns with self-reported poor memory at ages 50 to 65.

clusters.title: This tiny implant, smaller than a grain of salt, can read your brain
clusters.summary: A new neural implant is so small it can rest on a grain of salt, yet it can track and wirelessly transmit brain activity for over a year. It’s powered by laser light that safely passes through tissue and communicates using tiny infrared signals. This ultra-miniature device could transform how scientists study the brain without invasive wiring.

items.title: This tiny implant, smaller than a grain of salt, can read your brain

It’s powered by laser light that safely passes through tissue and communicates using tiny infrared signals. This ultra-miniature device could transform how scientists study the brain without invasive wiring.

clusters.title: Women who hate men: Study finds similarities in gendered hate speech on Reddit
clusters.summary: A new analysis of extremist Reddit communities reveals that online groups dedicated to hating men share identical language patterns and emotional characteristics with groups dedicated to hating women, suggesting toxicity transcends the gender of the perpetrator.

items.title: Women who hate men: Study finds similarities in gendered hate speech on Reddit
items.description: A new analysis of extremist Reddit communities reveals that online groups dedicated to hating men share identical language patterns and emotional characteristics with groups dedicated to hating women, suggesting toxicity transcends the gender of the perpetrator.

clusters.title: New AI tool predicts cancer spread with surprising accuracy
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered that cancer spread isn’t random—it follows a kind of biological “program.” By studying colon tumor cells, they identified gene patterns that signal whether a cancer is likely to metastasize. Their AI model, MangroveGS, can predict this risk with about 80% accuracy and even works across multiple cancer types. This could transform how doctors decide who needs aggressive treatment and who doesn’t.

items.title: New AI tool predicts cancer spread with surprising accuracy

This could transform how doctors decide who needs aggressive treatment and who doesn’t.

clusters.title: Audio tapes reveal mass rule-breaking in Milgram’s obedience experiments
clusters.summary: A fresh analysis of audio recordings from Stanley Milgram's infamous shock experiments reveals that his most obedient volunteers routinely broke the rules of the supposed scientific study.

items.title: Audio tapes reveal mass rule-breaking in Milgram’s obedience experiments
items.description: A fresh analysis of audio recordings from Stanley Milgram's infamous shock experiments reveals that his most obedient volunteers routinely broke the rules of the supposed scientific study.

clusters.title: Precision medicine may be on the way for patients with endometriosis
clusters.summary: Endometriosis is a devastating chronic pain condition caused by uterine-like cells growing where they aren't supposed to. People with endometriosis can develop extremely painful periods, bladder issues, and even infertility. Most people who are diagnosed with endometriosis are first treated with progesterone-based birth control.

items.title: Precision medicine may be on the way for patients with endometriosis
items.description: Endometriosis is a devastating chronic pain condition caused by uterine-like cells growing where they aren't supposed to. People with endometriosis can develop extremely painful periods, bladder issues, and even infertility. Most people who are diagnosed with endometriosis are first treated with progesterone-based birth control. But a third of all patients fail this therapy because it is ineffective for them while many others stop taking it due to side effects.

clusters.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
clusters.summary: Long COVID remains a frustrating medical mystery, affecting up to 1 in 10 people long after the initial infection fades. Now, scientists have uncovered a crucial clue hidden deep within the immune system. By analyzing individual immune cells, they identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells—especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID.
